coredata.dc:title: Fabricating dendritic N-C/MnOx to enable a highly efficient oxygen evolution reaction electrocatalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhai, Xiuhui
Wang, Xiang
Pang, Xinle
Zhang, Jun
Wu, Qiong
Na, Weidan
Zhou, Yingmei
Tian, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Exploiting effective and low-cost electrocatalysts toward water oxidation is of vital significance for boosting overall water splitting. Herein, we proposed a simple method for constructing dendritic N-doped carbon/ manganese oxides (N-C/MnOx) hybrid nanocomposites by annealing the composite of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and γ-MnOOH (γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs) at different temperatures under N2/O2 atmosphere. The obtained N-C/MnOx exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic activity and stability for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) owing to their unique dendritic structure, rich oxygen vacancies, optimized electronic structure, abundant Mn-C/N, and large electrochemical active area. Among of the prepared N-C/MnOx hybrid nanocomposites, N-C/Mn2O3 showed the best performance toward OER with an overpotential of merely 347 mV to reach to 10 mA cm−2, along with a small Tafel slope of 75.51 mV dec−1. More remarkably, by combining Pt/C with N-C/Mn2O3 for overall water splitting, a battery voltage of only 1.67 V is needed to reach the 10 mA cm−2, rivaling the activity of the state-of-the-art IrO2 ‖ Pt/C couple.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese oxides
N-CQDs
Oxygen evolution reaction
Electrocatalysis
Water spitting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation process of N-C/MnOx.

(a) TEM images of N-CQDs (inset: the diameter distribution of N-CQDs), (b-c) TEM images of N-C/Mn2O3 under different magnifications. (d) HRTEM image of N-C/Mn2O3 with SAED pattern in the inset. SEM images of (e) N-C/Mn2O3 and (f) Mn2O3. (g) XRD patterns of as-prepared nanomaterials.
XPS spectrum of (a) survey scan, (b) Mn 2p of Mn2O3, (c) (b) Mn 2p of N-C/Mn2O3, (d) O 1s of Mn2O3, and (e) O 1s of N-C/Mn2O3.
(a) LSV polarization curves, (b) Tafel slopes, (c) Nyquist plots, and (d) double layer current vs scan rates plots of Mn2O3, N-C/Mn2O3, N-C/MnO, N-C/MnO2.

(a) Schematic diagram of overall water splitting equipment, (b) LSV curves of overall water splitting. and (c) chronopotentiometry tests of the overall water splitting at 10 mA cm−2 of N-C/Mn2O3 || Pt/C couple.
XRD pattern (a) and SEM images (b) of N-C/Mn2O3 after CP tests.

head.ce:title: Fabricating dendritic N-C/MnOx to enable a highly efficient oxygen evolution reaction electrocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou 221018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou 221018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploiting effective and low-cost electrocatalysts toward water oxidation is of vital significance for boosting overall water splitting. Herein, we proposed a simple method for constructing dendritic N-doped carbon/ manganese oxides (N-C/MnOx) hybrid nanocomposites by annealing the composite of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and γ-MnOOH (γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs) at different temperatures under N2/O2 atmosphere. The obtained N-C/MnOx exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic activity and stability for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) owing to their unique dendritic structure, rich oxygen vacancies, optimized electronic structure, abundant Mn-C/N, and large electrochemical active area. Among of the prepared N-C/MnOx hybrid nanocomposites, N-C/Mn2O3 showed the best performance toward OER with an overpotential of merely 347 mV to reach to 10 mA cm−2, along with a small Tafel slope of 75.51 mV dec−1. More remarkably, by combining Pt/C with N-C/Mn2O3 for overall water splitting, a battery voltage of only 1.67 V is needed to reach the 10 mA cm−2, rivaling the activity of the state-of-the-art IrO2 ‖ Pt/C couple.

ce:section.ce:para: Converting the electrical energy that generated from the intermittent solar and wind into hydrogen fuels is regarded as an appealing strategy for mitigating the aggravated environmental issues and energy crisis [1-3]. Water splitting driven by electrical energy has been demonstrated to more effective in producing hydrogen compared with photocatalytic hydrogen production [4, 5]. However, there are also great difficulties for the practical application. Generally, electrochemical water splitting involves of two half reactions, that are anodic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [6]. The sluggish kinetics and uphill thermodynamics of the OER have been posed as one of the biggest challenges in water splitting [7]. At present, the Ru-/Ir-based oxides are regarded to be the best electrocatalysts for OER. Nevertheless, the scarce reserves and exorbitant price limited their commercialization [8-10]. Therefore, it is particularly urgent to develop efficient, low-cost, and abundant electrocatalysts for alleviating the kinetics and thermodynamics of OER to promote the practical application.

Recently, non-precious metal oxides have brought a lot of attention, because of their superior electrocatalytic activity for OER [11, 12]. For example, Ma et al. have synthesized the NiO nanoparticles and semi-conductive carbon nitride (CN), which have been demonstrated to be highly active toward OER [13]. They have demonstrated that the NiO and CN linked by a Ni-N bond possess a low Gibbs energy for OER intermediate adsorptions, which is favorable for facilitating the charge transfer and boosting electrocatalytic OER. Besides, with the development of novel synthesis technologies [14-17], Mn-based oxides showed a fascinating potential application in catalysis, sensor, and energy storage due to their non-toxicity, low-cost, structural flexibility, and a large number of unsaturated edge sites [14]. Among various Mn-based oxides, nanosized Mn2O3, with easily tuned morphologies (nanosheets, nanocubes, or nanoparticles), various valence states of manganese atoms and distorted crystal structure have been endowed with huge potential for electrocatalytic water oxidation [15]. However, an activity gap between Mn2O3 and other transition metal oxides (e.g. Co2O3, NiO) is still existed, mainly originating from its poor conductivity and unsatisfactory stability [16-18]. Therefore, it is urgent to exploit suitable strategy for fabricating the Mn2O3 to promote the elecrocatalytic OER performance to achieve the goal of large-scale H2 production.

Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), a category of zero-dimensional (0D) conductive nanomaterials, were widely studied and applied in the fields of sensors, biomaterials, photocatalysis because of their distinctive physicochemical properties [19-21]. In our previous work, it had been established that the N-CQDs could play an essential role in improving electrocatalytic water oxidation performance by accelerating the electron mobility and increasing the dispersity of materials due to their inherently good conductivity and abundant groups on the surface [22]. Our other works also showed that N-CQDs were capable to modulate the morphology and phase, due to abundant hydroxy groups on the surface, for which could serve as a reductant to form hydrogen bonding with O atom in MnO2 [23]. Nevertheless, the influences of the formation of metal-C/N on their OER performance are rarely reported [19]. Therefore, the researches on fabricating the metal-C/N and their influences on OER performance are necessary.

In consideration of above analysis coupled with our pervious works, we present a facile strategy for improving electrocatalytic OER activity of Mn2O3 via annealing the N-CQDs/γ-MnOOH composites, which may narrow the catalytic activity gap between Mn2O3 and Fe/Co/Ni oxides. Fortunately, a series of experiments established that the obtained Mn2O3 encapsulated by N-doped carbon framework that derived from N-CQDs efficiently could effectively improve the electrocatalytic OER performance. Benefiting from the unique dendritic structure, enhanced electrical conductivity, good dispersity, abundant oxygen vacancies, rich Mn-C/N bond, and plenty of electrocatalytic active sites originated from the porous structures, the as-obtained N-C/Mn2O3 showed excellent OER performance with merely 347 mV to drive OER to reach 10 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slopes of 75.51 mV dec−1. Additionally, by coupling the dendritic N-C/Mn2O3 with Pt/C, a voltage of merely 1.67 V is necessitated to drive the overall water splitting at 10 mA cm−2. Impressively, the voltage of N-C/Mn2O3 nearly keeps the same as the original one after long-time CP operation, implying the remarkably electrochemical stability. This work represented a reasonable strategy for developing efficient and low-cost N-doped-carbon/transition-metal-oxides with the N-CQDs aids.

Potassium hydroxide (KOH, Aladdin, AR, China), potassium permanganate (KMnO4, Macklin, AR, China), ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4, Aladdin, AR, China), ethyl acetate (CH3CH2OCOCH3, Macklin, AR, China), absolute enthanol (CH3CH2OH, Aladdin, AR, China), critric acid (CA, Macklin, AR, China), and Dicyandiamide (DCDA, Macklin, AR, China).

The N-CQDs was synthesis according to a previous reported method with a little modification [24]. In a typical synthesis, 0.9 g citric acid (CA) and 0.6 g dicyandiamide (DCDA) were mixed and grounded. After that, the obtained homogeneous mixture was heated in a tube furnace to 180°C with a rate of 10°C min−1, and kept for 2.0 h in a N2 carrier flowing. Subsequently, the sample was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min, and then collected by filtering through a filter membrane. Finally, the N-CQDs were obtained by lyophilized.

To obtain the γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs, the dentritic γ-MnOOH was prepared as equal (1) by a facile hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.158g of KMnO4 and 0.072 g (NH4)2C2O4 were dissolved into 15 mL of deionized (DI) water. 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added into the above mentioned solution under vigorous stirring for 10 min. Then, the homogeneous solution was transferred into 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave to maintain for 24h at 180 °C. Subsequently, the γ-MnOOH black powder was obtained consecutively through centrifugation, washed with a mixture of water and absolute ethanol, and then dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60°C for 6 h. The 0.05 g γ-MnOOH and 0.01 g N-CQDs were dissolved into 10 mL DI water, then kept for 6 h at 160°C in a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, Subsequently, the products were obtained by centrifugation, washed with 15 mL DI water for 3 times.
2(NH₄)₂C₂O₄+KMnO₄+H+→2CO₂+γ-MnOOH +2(NH₄)₂CO3

A two-step strategy was applied to fabricate the starfish-like N-C/Mn2O3 hybrid composites. As depicted in Scheme 1 , the γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs composites were firstly prepared by a simple hydrothermal method at 160°C for 6 h, where the freshly prepared N-CQDs and γ-MnOOH were used as original materials. Then the obtained γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs were further annealed in an tubular furnace at 750 °C for 2 h in a N2 flow, and the starfish-like N-C/Mn2O3 was successfully constructed. The N-C/MnOx was prepared by annealing the γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs with different temperature under N2/O2 atmosphere. Specifically, the N-C/Mn2O3 and N-C/MnO were synthesized in a N2 atmosphere at 750°C and 850°C, respectively. The N-C/MnO2 was obtained in a O2 atmosphere at 400°C. For comparison, the pure Mn2O3 was prepared by annealing the γ-MnOOH in a N2 atmosphere at 850°C.

The phases of the different catalysts were obtained by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku UIV Japan) with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). Scan electron microscopy image were captured by using a field emission scaning electron microscope (FESEM, FEG-XL30, USA). Transmission electron microscopy images were obtained on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2100, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra were established via using a X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, JEOL JPS-9010 MC, USA).

Electrochemical measurements were operated in a standard system of three-electrode by using an electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E, Shanghai, China), which employ carbon rod, NF (1 cm * 1 cm) that coated with as-obtained samples, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, counter electrode, and working electrode, respectively. The Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was applied to assess the OER activity of different samples. The Tafel slopes were evloved from the polarization curves with a scanning rate of 1 mV s−1. The electrochemical double layer capacitance (CdI) was calculated from Cyclic voltammetry (CV), which carried out at different scanning rates. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) can be deduced by the equation: ECSA ≈ Cdl / Cs, where the Cs is the specific capacitance of the samples in the same electrolyte. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were operated in a range of 1 Hz-1000 kHz with a sinusoidal perturbation of 5 mA.

A two-step strategy was applied to fabricate the starfish-like N-C/MnOx hybrid composites. As depicted in Scheme 1, the γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs composites were firstly prepared by a simple hydrothermal method at 160°C for 6 h, where the freshly prepared N-CQDs and γ-MnOOH were used as original materials. Then the obtained γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs were further annealed in an tubular furnace at 750 °C, 850 °C for 2 h in a N2 flow and 400 °C for 2h in a O2 flow, the starfish-like N-C/Mn2O3, N-C/MnO and N-C/MnO2 were successfully constructed.

The morphology of the synthesized samples was recorded by employing TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, N-CQDs exhibited a quasi-spherical structure with diameter of only 1.5-2.5 nm (in the inset of Fig. 1a), indicating that the N-CQDs were successfully synthesized. Fig. S1a depicted that the prepared γ-MnOOH exhibited the starfish-like morphology, and the γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs kept the same shape as the γ-MnOOH (Fig. S2b). From Fig. 1b, it is clearly observed that the N-C/Mn2O3 can still keep the dendritic structure after the calcination at 750°C for 2 h, which benefits for the mobility of mass and charge [25]. After a careful observation, we can find the surface of Mn2O3 surrounded by N-doped carbon dots, which can effectively maintain the structure and activity in electrocatalysis [26]. Furthermore, we can also observe that the surface of N-C/Mn2O3 exits abundant cave compared with γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs. The cave came from the release of carbon dioxide, which generated from carboxyl group of the surface of N-CQDs in the annealing process (Fig. S2) [27]. TEM images of other products were recorded as Fig. S3. As seen, both the N-C/MnO, and N-C/MnO2 exhibited the starfish-like structure, which is similar to the N-C/Mn2O3. However, from the Fig. S3c, it is clearly observed that the Mn2O3 possess the nanorod-like structure with rough surface. These results have implied that the N-CQDs played a key role in stabilizing the structure of Mn2O3 [28]. The HRTEM image clearly showed that the dendritic Mn2O3 were surrounded by carbon matrix (Fig .1d), wherein the lattice spacing of 0.223 nm and 0.271 nm in the outer layer were assigned to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of graphite carbon phase, respectively [29, 30], while the relatively dark Mn2O3 was found with a lattice distance of 0.476 nm, ascribing to the (200) plane of bixbyite Mn2O3 [31], The SAED pattern showed intense diffractions spots that could be appointed to the (200) planes of Mn2O3, (022) and (222) planes of graphite carbon. SEM was carried out to explore the surface morphology of N-C/Mn2O3 and Mn2O3. Fig. 1e uncovered that the surface of N-C/Mn2O3 existed a representative star-like structure with rough surface, which was in accordance with the observation in the TEM images. The distinctive nanostructure was beneficial for exposing the more electrocatalytic active site as compared to Mn2O3 with smooth surface (Fig. 1f) [32].

To investigate the crystal phase of different materials, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of different nanomaterials were also recorded. As showed in Fig. 1g, a broad XRD peak located at the around 30° was in good agreement with the XRD pattern of N-CQDs, manifesting the successful fabrication of amorphous N-CQDs [32]. The diffraction peaks of γ-MnOOH and γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs well matched with the standard γ-phase MnOOH (PDF # 41-1379) [33]. Meanwhile, a little shift can be observed at different rates of γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs compared with the peak of γ-MnOOH, which was arisen from the efficient electron transfer between γ-MnOOH and N-CQDs [34]. The XRD patterns of N-C/MnO2, prepared by annealing the γ-MnOOH@N-CQDs at 400°C in O2 atmosphere, can readily be corresponded to the MnO2 (PDF # 24-0735) [35]. The diffraction peaks of N-C/MnO were well matched with the standard MnO (PDF # 07-0230) [36]. And the diffraction peaks of N-C/Mn2O3 and Mn2O3 were corresponded to the standard Mn2O3 (PDF # 41-1442) [37]. After careful analysis, we can also discover that the peaks of N-C/Mn2O3 are also slightly shifted by the 2θ degree of 0.1° compared with Mn2O3 due to the residual stress, which may be beneficial for boosting electrocatalytic reactions [38].

The FT-IR was conducted to uncover the surface functional groups of N-CQDs, N-C/Mn2O3 and Mn2O3. As showed in Fig. S4a, the FT-IR spectrum of the calcined N-CQDs occupied plenty of distinct peaks including oxygen/nitrogen functional group. The peaks around 1108, 1340, and 1583 cm−1 are assigned to C-O-C, O-H bending, and stretching vibration of C=O, respectively. The peak at 2931 cm−1 is ascribed to stretching vibration of CH2. The N-H and O-H stretching vibration are observed at 3450 and 3511 cm−1, respectively. The typical peaks around 410, 478, and 606 cm−1 were appointed to Mn-O-Mn stretching vibration, Mn-O vibration, and Mn-O symmetric stretching, respectively, while the peaks located at 1651 cm−1 were assigned to O-H bending vibration originated from the surface functional groups of Mn2O3. Obviously, the FT-IR spectrum of N-C/Mn2O5 also showed C-O-C vibration at around 1104 cm−1, indicating the formation of the composites of N-doped carbon and Mn2O3. To verify the FT-IR analysis, Raman spectrum of N-CQDs, N-C/Mn2O3 and Mn2O3 were further obtained. As shown in Fig. S4b, the peaks of N-CQDs around at 1351 and 1590 cm−1 were ascribed to the D (disorder) and G (graphene) band, in which the ID/IG is 1.05, indicating the formation of N-CQDs with abundant N-C defects, benefiting for the improvement of conductivity of composites. The representative peaks at 315, 371, and 658 cm−1 were assigned to out-of-plane bending vibration, asymmetric stretching vibration of Mn-O-Mn, and Mn-O symmetric stretching vibration, respectively. And the other peaks around at 1107 and 1300 cm−1 were ascribed to the D and G band, the shift of both peaks compared with N-CQDs was resulted from the synergistic effect between N-C and Mn2O3, manifesting the successful formation of N-C/Mn2O3 composites.

To explore the surface chemical composition and chemical state of N-C/Mn2O3 and Mn2O3, XPS tests were carried out and the corresponding patterns are revealed in Fig. 2 . As depicted in Fig. 2a, the full-spectrum uncovered the coexisting of Mn, C, and O on the surface of N-C/Mn2O3 [39]. However, very weak N 1s peak can be observed in the survey scan, which is originated from the low N content in the N-C/Mn2O3 hybrid [40]. As showed in Fig. 2b, the Mn 2p spectrum of Mn2O3 was deconvoluted into six sharp peaks, the peaks at 641.06 and 652.33 eV were assigned to Mn2+ 2p3/2 and Mn2+ 2p1/2, respectively [41], the peaks at 642.08 and 653.49 eV were ascribed to Mn3+ (2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively) [42], while the other two peaks at 643.23 and 654.61 eV were corresponded to Mn4+ (2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively) [43]. These analyses have confirmed the coexistence of Mn2+, Mn3+, and Mn4+ in N-C/Mn2O3.

As showed in Fig. 2c, the Mn 2P of N-C/Mn2O3 could also deconvoluted into Mn2+, Mn3+, Mn4+ of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn2+, Mn3+, Mn4+ of Mn 2p1/2. Further observation indicated that the new peak around 644.48 eV was ascribed to the Mn-N/C [44, 45], which was beneficial for the mobility of electron in the electrocatalytic process [46]. We also clearly observe that the Mn3+ of Mn 2p1/2 in N-C/Mn2O3 is weaker compared with the Mn3+ of bare Mn2O3, maybe resulting from the reduction of Mn3+ to Mn2+ by N-CQDs in the annealing process [47]. Moreover, there is a clear negatively shift of Mn3+ in the N-C/Mn2O3 compared with bare Mn2O3, indicating the introduction of N-CQDs could modulate the electronic structure. Fig. 2d showed that the O 1s of Mn2O3 could be splitted into two peaks, which were assigned to Mn-O-H (531.31 eV) and Mn-O-Mn (529.85 eV), respectively [48, 49]. In Fig. 2e, the O 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into various peaks owing to the surface of N-CQDs and Mn2O3 contains abundant oxygen species, the typical peaks at 531.27 eV and 529.81 eV were assigned to the Mn-O-H and Mn-O-Mn, while the peaks lied at 532.54 eV and 530.27 eV can be appointed to the Mn-O-C and C-O-C/C-OH, respectively, implying the successful fabrication of N-C/Mn2O3 [50]. Significantly, the peaks at 532.01 eV was ascribed to the oxygen vacancies, resulting from the reducing property of N-CQDs [51].

Inspired by the unique dendritic starfish-like structure [52], rich porous surface [53], abundant oxygen vacancies [54], Mn-N/C bond and synergistic effect between Mn2O3 and N-CQDs, such N-C/Mn2O3 are suitably predicted to have excellent electrocatalytic OER performance. Take it into consideration, the electrochemical OER tests were carried out on a typical three-electrode cell in 1.0 M KOH. For the sake of contrast, the OER performance of NF, N-C/MnO2, N-C/MnO, and Mn2O3 were also recorded under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 a, all sample-modified electrodes showed oxidation peaks between 1.30 V to 1.45 V, even the anodic current of some modified electrodes exceed 10 mA cm−2 within the potential ranges, but no bubbles were observed on the surface of electrodes, which originated from the oxidation of Ni2+ to Ni3+ in the NF [55]. All of the modified electrodes showed better electrocatalytic activity than NF. We also noticed the N-C/Mn2O3 exhibited the best electrocatalytic performance while the Mn2O3 had a larger overpotential at 10 mA•cm−2, implying N-C derived from N-CQDs could effectively enhance the electrocatalytic OER activity. The N-C/Mn2O3 also showed a lowest onset potential of 1.55 V, being 20, 30, 50, and 60 mV lower than N-C/MnO (1.57 V), Mn2O3 (1.68 V), N-C/MnO2 (1.60 V), and NF (1.61 V) NF (Fig. S5), respectively, indicating the N-C/Mn2O3 can easily trigger OER [56]. On the other hand, for visually comparing their electrochemical activity for OER, Fig. S6 listed the overpotentials of all modified electrodes at 10 mA•cm−2 in the form of bar diagram. As showed, the N-C/Mn2O3 showed excellent electrocatalytic activity with a low overpotential of 346 mV to drive 10 mA•cm−2, which was 21, 39, 48, and 66 mV lower than those of N-C/MnO (367 mV), Mn2O3 (385 mV), N-C/MnO2 (394 mV), and NF (411 mV), respectively. To our knowledge, the N-C/Mn2O3 occupied the relatively lower overpotential at 10 mA cm−2 according to reported excellent Mn2O3-based electrocatalysts for water oxidation (Table S1).

In order to obtain the kinetic understanding of the OER, the Tafel slopes of the N-C/Mn2O3, N-C/MnO, Mn2O3, and N-C/MnO2 were evolved from their LSV curves (Fig. 3b), where the N-C/Mn2O3 exhibited the smallest Tafel slope of 90.7 mV dec−1, suggesting an available OER kinetic of N-C/Mn2O3 electrocatalyst [57]. Moreover, Fig. 3c showed the EIS curves of different electrocatalysts, wherein the N-C/manganese oxides exhibited smaller curvature than Mn2O3, indicating excellent capacity for accelerating charge transfer, which stemmed from the interactions between manganese-based oxides and N-doped carbon derived from N-CQDs [58]. Furthermore, the electrocatalytic active surface areas (ECSAs) evolved from double layer-capacitance (Cdl) were obtained to uncover the origin of better OER activity (Fig. 3d). As depicted in Fig. 4 d, the Cdl value of N-C/Mn2O3 was 2.39 mF cm−2, which was larger than other electrocatalysts, resulting from porous structure caused by the N-CQDs [59].

Apart from eletrocatalytic activity, the long-time durability is another crucial criterion for assessing OER performance. In this regard, the long-time chronopotentiometry (CP) technique was applied for evaluating the stability of N-C/Mn2O3. As displayed in Fig. 3e, the N-C/Mn2O3 could maintain the eletrocatalytic activity with negligible variation after continuous 24 h tests at the current density of 10 mA cm−2. For comparison, the unmodified pristine Mn2O3 were equipped for CP test, we can easily find that the activity of the Mn2O3 without outer carbon layer dramatically decrease just an hour later, significantly indicating the outer carbon layer formed by annealing the N-CQDs plays a key role in keeping the original activity [60].

Motivated by the good OER activity, the N-C/Mn2O3 was also highly predicted to occupy remarkable performance for water electrolysis [61]. Therefore, a two-electrode system, that employed N-C/Mn2O3 and Pt/C as anode cathode, respectively, was applied to study electrocatalytic OER performance of N-C/Mn2O3 (Fig. 4a). Interestingly, the N-C/Mn2O3 ‖ Pt/C electrode couple merely requires 1.67 V to reach 10 mA cm−2, even rivaling the IrO2 ‖ Pt/C couple (1.62 V) (Fig. 4b). As showed in Fig. 4c, the potential can keep for at least 22 h with a negligible change, suggesting that the as-prepared dendritic N-C/Mn2O3 possess a great potential toward water electrolysis [62].

To verify the stability of catalyst structure, the SEM and XRD were conducted to explore the morphology and phase of spent N-C/Mn2O3 catalyst. Fig. 5 a showed the SEM images. More importantly, the characteristic diffraction peaks of spent N-C/Mn2O3 were still accordance with the fresh N-C/Mn2O3, implying the stability of phase even undergone long-term electrocatalytic process, which maybe originated from the N-doped carbon armour in the surface of Mn2O3 [63]. However, the diffraction peaks of (211) crystal plane of spent N-C/Mn2O3 was almost disappeared, maybe indicating the (211) crystal plane was electrocatalytic activity crystal plane [64]. After a long-time CP tests, the surface of N-C/Mn2O3 becomes rougher due to the surface of materials is the key field of electrocatalytic OER. More importantly, as shown in Fig. 5b, we can also find the N-C/Mn2O3 nearly keep the initial dendritic morphology, suggesting the N-C armor can effectively the stability of morphology [65].

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile route for constructing nanocomposites materials with manganese oxides and N-doped carbon derived from N-CQDs by annealing. Systematic study revealed that the N-CQDs played a key role in improving the electrochemical OER performance of Mn2O3. The reasons of remarkable enhancement in OER performance induced by N-CQDs are resulted to following aspects: (1) improving the electrical conductivity of Mn2O3; (2) increasing the number of active sites by making hole; (3) optimizing the electronic structure of Mn3+ in the N-C/Mn2O3; (4) improving the catalytic performance of each active sites by forming Mn-N/C between Mn2O3 and N-CQDs; (5) the strong synergistic effect between different components in the composites is also conducive to the large promotion of OER performance. The obtained starfish-like N-C/Mn2O3 exhibited good OER activity in terms of a low overpotential of merely 346 mV to reach to 10 mA cm−2, even surpassing most of the reported excellent Mn2O3-based electrocatalyst. Remarkably, by combining the N-C/Mn2O3 with Pt/C, the device merely requires 1.67 V to drive the overall water electrolysis to achieve 10 mA cm−2. More importantly, the N-C/Mn2O3 also exhibited excellent durability; the variation of potential was nearly neglectable after 22 h CP test. We exhibited a favorable method for building N-C/Mn2O3 with porous structure, abundant Mn-N/C, rich oxygen vacancy with the aid of N-CQDs. However, the role of N-CQDs on improving the OER activity by regulating the electronic structure is still not clear, this is maybe future plans for our research. Our current work may provide guidance for improving the electrocatalytic performance of transition metal composites by coupling with appropriate N-CQDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: I hereby declare, on behalf of myself and my co-authors, that: the paper submitted is an original work and not under consideration for publication by any other journal, and that all authors are aware of the submission and agree to its publication. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Authors acknowledgement the financial supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (Grant No. 19KJB150019), Youth Science and Technology Talents Enrollment Project of the Jiangsu Association of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2021.07.025.
